questions.question: What mindset does fault-tolerant design require about components?
options: Assume every component will fail, and design so each failure is absorbed rather than causing an outage
Only worry about failures after they happen
questions.explanation: At scale, something is always failing — servers, disks, networks, whole data centers. Fault tolerance treats failure as the normal case and ensures no single failure becomes a user-visible outage. (Netflix's Chaos Monkey even kills servers on purpose to prove it.)

questions.question: What is a single point of failure (SPOF)?
options: A component that fails most often
Any component whose failure alone takes down the whole system
The slowest component in the system
A component with no backups for its data
questions.explanation: A SPOF is anything that, if it fails by itself, causes a system-wide outage — e.g. a single load balancer in front of redundant servers. Finding and removing SPOFs (via redundancy) is the first task of fault-tolerant design.

questions.question: Three services each 99.9% available are chained in series (a request needs all three). What is the combined availability?
options: About 99.7% — availabilities multiply, so the chain is less available than any part
questions.explanation: In series, a request needs every component up, so availabilities multiply: 0.999^3 ≈ 0.997 = 99.7%. Each added dependency drags availability down, which is why long dependency chains are fragile.

questions.question: Two servers each 99% available run in parallel (only one needed). What's the combined availability?
options: 99.99% — both-down probability is 0.01 × 0.01 = 0.0001, so the pair is far more available than one
questions.explanation: In parallel you only need one up, so the FAILURE probabilities multiply: both down = 0.01 × 0.01 = 0.0001 (0.01%), giving 99.99% availability. This is the core case for redundancy — two cheap independent parts beat one expensive one. It assumes failures are independent.

questions.question: Why must redundant copies be placed in different failure domains (racks, AZs, regions)?
options: Because copies in the same domain share dependencies (power, network), so one event takes them all down — the availability math assumes independent failures
It doesn't matter where copies are placed
questions.explanation: Redundancy only helps if copies fail independently. Two replicas in the same rack share a power supply and switch, so one rack failure kills both. Spreading across failure domains (separate AZs/regions) ensures a single event can't take out all copies — preserving the independence the math assumes.
